title: Dog Bleeding from One Nostril (Epistaxis): Triage and Home Care Guide
meta_desc: Is your dog bleeding from one nostril? Learn if it is an emergency, common causes, and how photos help your veterinarian diagnose the issue.
content_html:
What is Epistaxis?

Bleeding from the nose, or epistaxis, occurs when the small blood vessels within the nasal passages rupture. When the bleeding is restricted to just one nostril (unilateral), it typically indicates that the source of the problem is localized to that specific side of the nose. This can be caused by anything from minor trauma and foreign objects to infections or dental issues affecting the upper jaw.

Is it an emergency?

The urgency level for a single bloody nostril is generally considered Medium. While it may not require an immediate midnight trip to the ER, your dog should be seen by a veterinarian within 12 to 24 hours. However, if the bleeding is heavy and will not stop, if your dog's gums appear pale, or if they are acting extremely lethargic, you should seek emergency veterinary care immediately.

Why a photo helps triage

Taking a clear photo or a short video of the bleeding can be a vital tool for your veterinary team. Because bleeding often starts and stops, a photo allows the vet to see the volume of blood lost, the exact shade (bright red versus dark or pinkish), and whether there is any visible swelling or discharge. This visual evidence helps the vet determine the severity of the issue before they even begin their physical examination.

faqs.question: Can I use a cold compress to stop the bleeding?
faqs.answer: Yes, you can gently apply a cold pack wrapped in a towel to the bridge of your dog's nose to help constrict blood vessels, provided it does not cause them additional stress.

faqs.question: Could a foreign object be the cause?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is common for dogs to sniff up foreign material like grass seeds or foxtails, which can irritate the nasal lining and cause one-sided bleeding.

faqs.question: Should I tilt my dog's head back?
faqs.answer: No, you should keep your dog's head level. Tilting the head back can cause blood to run down the throat, leading to gagging or even aspiration into the lungs.
